title: TCP Connections, Accept Loops, Half-Closes, Deadlines, and Failure · Go Semantics Case: Tcp Reliable Stream Model
summary: A raw server treats TCP as messages, leaks accepted connections, uses no deadlines, retries ambiguous writes, and cannot distinguish peer close, timeout, reset, or local shutdown. Planned artifact: Add and verify the tcp reliable stream model increment in bounded TCP connection lifecycle state machine. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Explain ordered reliable byte-stream delivery while accounting for segmentation, retransmission, flow control, congestion, and missing message boundaries.
Prove the same bounded message result for every legal segmentation of an identical byte sequence.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Predict Tcp Reliable Stream Model · Theory Tcp Reliable Stream Model
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory protocol team handles case 3383 in a signed webhook receiver behind an HTTP/2 to HTTP/1.1 gateway. Build deterministic Go evidence to classify connection, deadline, completion, and close state without inventing message boundaries; preserve this operating constraint: one connection has exactly one read owner and one terminal close owner; then lower one byte or time budget and prove rejection occurs before allocation or dispatch. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Tcp Reliable Stream Model contract for the supplied data.

Evidence target: Predict Tcp Reliable Stream Model · Theory Tcp Reliable Stream Model.
